elements.sent-2411-001: Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has become a promising paradigm for collaborative training with distributed and private graph data.
elements.sent-2411-002: One-shot Federated Learning (OFL) enables collaboration in a single communication round to largely reduce communication costs and potential security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing OFL methods are not designed for graph data and existing FGL methods are ineffective within one communication round under both data and model heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-004: To mitigate this gap, we are the first to propose a one-shot personalized federated graph learning method for node classification, which is also compatible with the Secure Aggregation scheme.
elements.sent-2411-005: We estimate and aggregate the statistics of class-wise feature distribution to generate a global pseudo-graph on the server, which could be used to train a global graph model.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, We reveal the under-explored problem of existing personalized FGL methods that their personalized models are biased and neglect the ability to generalize to minorities.
elements.sent-2411-007: To achieve better personalization and generalization simultaneously, we propose a two-stage personalized training to adaptively utilize the personal information from local data and global information from the global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-008: Comprehensive experiments on 8 multi-scale graph datasets under different partitions with various settings demonstrate our superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines.

elements.sent-2411-009: Graphs are widely used in various fields to model the complex relations between entities, including recommendations~\cite{he2023simplifying, zhou2023contrastive, cai2023lightgcl , finance~\cite{yang2021financial, qiu2023default, hyun2023anti , and biomedicine~\cite{pfeifer2022gnn, qu2023graph, bang2023biomedical .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Most research works propose various algorithms~\cite{gasteiger2018predict, iscen2019label and models~\cite{wu2020comprehensive, zhang2019heterogeneous to analyze the graph data effectively, but they presuppose a centralized setting where data are collected and stored together.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the process of data collection can be both costly and impractical, especially in privacy-sensitive and competitive contexts, where the graph data may inadvertently reveal personal identity information~\cite{voigt2017eu, ji2016graph, li2023private, peng2022fedni, wu2023federated or confidential trade secrets~\cite{hubscher2022graph, fu2022federated .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: To address the dilemma between the need for a large volume of data and the increasing demand for privacy protection, federated graph learning (FGL) has become a predominant solution to train models among distributed graph data collaboratively.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, conventional FGL methods need numerous client-server communication rounds for optimization, which would incur many concerns including 1) security concerns: iterative communication could be vulnerable to potential man-in-the-middle attacks~\cite{wang2020man,yin2021see,mothukuri2021survey,zhu2024evaluating , 2) communication cost concerns: numerous upload-download operations could be a heavy burden for some clients~\cite{li2020federated, kairouz2021advances, dai2022dispfl and increase the risks of connection drop errors~\cite{qian2024dropfl .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: One-shot federated learning (OFL) reduces the communication to one round to address the above issues.
elements.sent-2411-015: It circumvents potential man-in-the-middle attacks and largely reduces the communication burden on local devices and the central server~\cite{guha2019one, zhang2022dense .}
elements.sent-2411-016: With only a single communication, collaborative training could be quickly adopted for distributed data.
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, OFL is more practical and naturally suitable for the current model market scenarios (e.g. ModelDB~\cite{vartak2016modeldb , TFX~\cite{baylor2017tfx , and Hugging Face~\cite{wolf2019huggingface ), with heterogeneous model~\cite{wang2023data .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In a word, OSFL is a promising solution to achieve a better utility-privacy-efficiency trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, considering that the nodes and topology of graph data are high heterogeneous, both existing FGL methods and OFL methods have limitations in graph learning in one shot.
elements.sent-2411-020: Existing FGL methods~\cite{zhang2021subgraph, baek2023personalized, huang2024federated, li2024fedgta, zhu2024fedtad rely on the many round communication for iterative model optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Most of them cannot support heterogeneous models since they rely on the aggregation of model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-022: And existing OFL methods are not compatible with graph data.
elements.sent-2411-023: The ensemble-based OFL methods~\cite{zhang2022dense, kang2023one, diao2023towards, zeng2024one can invert images from ensemble models, but face challenges in generating graph data because nodes in a graph are not independent.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The generative-based OFL methods~\cite{heinbaugh2023data, yang2024feddeo, yang2024exploring rely on pre-trained generative models (e.g. Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high ), which are not suitable for graph generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: They all need to upload their model parameters or side information independently and the operations on the server are not simply weighted average, making them less practical and privacy-preserving.
elements.sent-2411-026: The local graph data are highly imbalanced from both quantity and topology perspective.
elements.sent-2411-027: The personalized models are easily biased toward major class (with large quantities and high homophily to form compact communities) while neglecting other classes (with small amounts and surrounded with nodes from major classes), resulting in performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Fig.\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor illustrates the phenomenon empirically that there is severe performance degradation of existing personalized federated learning methods on minor classes.}
elements.sent-2411-029: To solve the (L1) and (L2), we first propose a One-shot Federated Graph Learning (OFGL) method to fill the blank between OFL and FGL, which also supports model heterogeneity and Secure Aggregation scheme Specifically, clients upload the estimated statistics of aggregated feature distribution rather than model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then the server aggregates the statistics with secure aggregation and generates a global pseudo-graph by matching the estimated distribution.
elements.sent-2411-031: The server distributes the global pseudo-graph and clients perform personalized model training with both local graph data and the global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-032: The whole process does not involve model parameters upload and download and the clients do not have to reveal their model.
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, the risks of model stealing could be eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-034: Furthermore, to solve the (L3) that ensuring a better personalization while avoiding degradation on minor classes, we propose a two-stage adaptive personalized model training.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the first stage, each client trains a global model with the global pseudo-graph from scratch, which is used as both a fixed teacher model and the initialization of the second stage training, In the second stage, each client fine-tunes the global model with local graph data.
elements.sent-2411-036: Meanwhile, we identify the minor and blended nodes from the perspective of both quantity and topology and then use the fixed copy of the global model to perform node-adaptive distillation for utilizing the global knowledge adaptively.
elements.sent-2411-037: The fine-tuned model could possess personalization ability from personal graph data while maintain local generalization ability from the global information in the global model.
elements.sent-2411-038: We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate our method on seven graph datasets across various settings, including 1) different dataset scales which range from thousands of nodes to millions of nodes, 2) two partitions: Louvain-based label imbalance partition and Metis-based label imbalance partition, 3) two learning paradigm: transductive and inductive learning, 4) different model configuration: model homophily and model heterogeneity, 5) two evaluation metrics: accuracy and F1-macro.
elements.sent-2411-039: The experimental results demonstrate the advantages of our method in one-shot federated graph learning under all settings.
elements.sent-2411-040: Further analysis shows that our adaptive personalized training achieves a better balance of personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-041: To sum up, our contributions are listed as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-042: To the best of our knowledge, we first investigate the one-shot federated graph learning problem under both data and model heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-043: We also connect the personalized training and graph node imbalance to reveal the under-explored model bias problems.
elements.sent-2411-044: We propose a novel method that first aggregates distribution from clients to generate global pseudo-graph, then distributes it to clients for two-stage personalized model training with node adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our method is compatible with Secure Aggregation and protects the model intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-046: We conduct comprehensive experiments on 8 graph datasets.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both accuracy and F1-macro evaluations in all settings.

elements.sent-2411-048: With the rapid development of federated learning methods, recent works introduce federated graph learning to collaboratively train graph models (e.g. GCN~\cite{kipf2016semi ) in a privacy-preserving manner and apply federated graph learning to many applications~\cite{wu2021fedgnn, zhang2024gpfedrec, yan2024federated, tang2024personalized, chen2021fede .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: From the graph level, each client possess multiple completely disjoint graphs (e.g. molecular graphs).
elements.sent-2411-050: Recent works~\cite{xie2021federated, tan2023federated mainly focus on the intrinsic heterogeneity among graphs from different clients.}
elements.sent-2411-051: From the sub-graph level, the graph possessed by each client can be regarded as a part of a larger global graph.
elements.sent-2411-052: To solve the problem of heterogeneous graph data, GraphFL~\cite{wang2022graphfl combines the federated learning and meta learning for the better generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-053: FedGL~\cite{chen2024fedgl uploads the node embedding and prediction for global supervison, but face heavy communication burden and potential privacy concerns.}
elements.sent-2411-054: FedPUB~\cite{baek2023personalized proposes to generate random graphs to measure the heterogeneity of graphs and conducts adaptive weight mask for better personalization.}
elements.sent-2411-055: FGSSL~\cite{huang2024federated conducts graph augmentation to mitigate the data heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-056: FedGTA~\cite{li2024fedgta connects the scalable graph learning and proposes topology-aware personalized optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-057: AdaFGL~\cite{li2024adafgl studies the problems under structure non-IID.}
elements.sent-2411-058: FGGP~\cite{wan2024federated constructs a global view by portotypes to conduct contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-059: FedTAD~\cite{zhu2024fedtad utilized the ensemble local models perform data-free distillation on the server.}
elements.sent-2411-060: To solve the missing connections between graphs of clients, FedSage~\cite{zhang2021subgraph and FedDEP~\cite{zhang2024deep additionally train a neighborhood generator to complete the missing edges.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: FedGCN~\cite{yao2024fedgcn additionally uploads and downloads the encrypted neighbor features to supplement the features of neighborhood.}
elements.sent-2411-062: One-shot federated learning largely reduce the communication costs and potential man-in-the-middle attacks.
elements.sent-2411-063: Mainstream OSFL methods can be categorized into 3 categories.
elements.sent-2411-064: DENSE~\cite{zhang2022dense utilized the model inversion to generate fake images from the direct ensemble for distillation.}
elements.sent-2411-065: FedOV~\cite{diao2023towards introduces the placeholders in model prediction layer.}
elements.sent-2411-066: IntactOFL~\cite{zeng2024one trains a gated MoE network by the generated fake images.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Co-Boost~\cite{dai2024enhancing further propose to optimize the fake images and ensemble weights iteratively.}
elements.sent-2411-068: FedDISC~\cite{yang2024exploring and FedDEO~\cite{yang2024feddeo utilize the pre-trained Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high to generate images conditioned on text or soft prompts to mitigate the data heterogeneity in federated learning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, existing OSFL methods are mainly designed for image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-070: They are either incompatible or ineffective for graph learning.
elements.figure-2411-004: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/overall_framework.pdf}} \caption{The pipeline of our proposed method, which consists of 5 steps: 1. feature distribution estimation on the client-side, 2. upload statistics of class-wise feature distribution to the server, 3. global pseudo-graph generation on the server-side, 4. server distributes the global pseudo-graph, 5. adaptive personalized training on the client-side. The process of uploading and aggregation of statistics from clients could be protected by Secure Aggregation scheme.} \label{fig:overall}
elements.sent-2411-071: Consider $m$ clients participating in the federated graph learning, where each client possesses its local undirected graph.
elements.sent-2411-072: The $k$-th client possesses the graph $G_k(\mathcal{V _k, \mathcal{E _k)$ with $|\mathcal{E _k|$ edges and $|\mathcal{V _k| = N_k$ nodes with the node features matrix is $\mathbf{X _k = \{\mathbf{x _1, ..., \mathbf{x _{n_k}\} \in \mathbb{R ^{|\mathcal{V _k| \times d}$, where $d$ is the dimension of node features and $\mathbf{x_i}$ denotes the feature of node $v_i$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The labels of this local graph are $\mathbf{Y _k = \{y_1,..., y_{n_k}\} \in \mathbb{R ^{|\mathcal{V _k|}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: In the semi-supervised node classification task, nodes on client $k$ are partitioned into labeled nodes $\mathcal{V _{k, L}$ and unlabeled nodes $\mathcal{V _{k, U}$, and the labels are also partitioned as $\mathbf{Y _{k, L}$ and $\mathbf{Y _{k, U}$ correspondingly.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Nodes in all clients' local graphs are categorized into $C$ distinct classes.
elements.sent-2411-077: A GNN model $M$ generally takes the node feature matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and the normalized adjacency matrix $\hat{\mathbf{A }$ as input and output the logits prediction $o \in \mathbb{R ^C$, which can be formulated as $o = M(\hat{\mathbf{A , \mathbf{X )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: For the prediction of a single node $v_i$, we shorten its formulation as $o_i = M(\mathbf{x_i )$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: We formulate the problem of one-shot personalized federated graph learning for node classification as: \begin{equation}\min \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} L(M_k, \mathbf{A}_k, \mathbf{X}_k, \mathbf{Y}_{k, U}), \label{eq:overall_obj_func}\end{equation} where we assume $m$ clients participating and each client has its local graph data $G_k$ and trains its personalized model $M_k$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Our goal is to minimize the objective Eq.~\ref{eq:overall_obj_func in a single upload-download communication round.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Consider a node $v_i \in \mathcal{V}$ in an undirected graph $G(\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$, the node degree $d(v_i)$ is the number of the edges connected with $v_i$, i.e. $d(v_i) = |\{e_{v_i, u |, u \in \mathcal{V}\}|$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Consider a labeled node $v_i \in \mathcal{V _L$ in an undirected graph $G(\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$, we calculate the node homophily $h_{node (v_i)$ within labeled nodes as: \begin{equation}h_{node}(v_i) = \frac{\left|\{u | (u \in \mathcal{N}_{v_i} \cap \mathcal{V}_L) \wedge (y_{v_i}=y_u) \}\right|}{|\mathcal{N}_{v_i} \cap \mathcal{V}_L|}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In undirected graph $G(V, E)$, we calculate the accumulated class homophily of class c as: \begin{equation}H(c) = \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}_c^{G}} h_{node}(u), \label{eq:H(c)}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N _c^{G = \{v_i \in \mathcal{V _L | y_i = c\}$ denotes the set of nodes belonging to class $c$ in $G$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Note that $H(c)$ considers both the number of nodes and the topology of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-086: A higher $H(c)$ would indicate a better ability of a vanilla GNN model to predict the nodes of class $c$ because of the large number and high homophily of nodes in this class.
elements.sent-2411-088: With Accumulated Class Homophily, we calculate the Class-aware Distillation Factor as: \begin{equation}w_{dist}(c) = \frac{1}{1+\log(H(c) + 1)}. \label{eq:w_dist}\end{equation} Classes with lower $H(c)$ would need more distillation from a balanced global model.
elements.sent-2411-089: We organize them as a vector $\mathbf{w_{dist }\in \mathbb{R ^C$ where $\mathbf{w_{dist (i) = w_{dist (i)$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-093: Then the server generates a small-size global pseudo-graph (including adjacent matrix and node features) by matching the recovered distribution; 4) The server distributes the generated global pseudo-graph to all clients; 5) Each client conducts the two-stage local personalized model training with both local graph data and the downloaded global pseudo-graph to obtain a model with better personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-094: Specifically, in the first step, we propose a Homophily-guided Reliable node Expansion strategy (HRE) as an augmentation to obtain more precise statistics of distribution.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the third step, we transform the techniques from graph condensation to server-side graph generation.
elements.sent-2411-096: In the fifth step, we recognize that existing personalized training often neglects the minor and blended nodes in local graph data, and the resulting model is biased towards the dominant nodes.
elements.sent-2411-097: We propose the two-stage personalized model training with node adaptive distillation for better personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-098: The concrete elaborations are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-099: We first propose to estimate the class-wise feature distribution locally on each client.
elements.sent-2411-100: On client $k$, we first calculate the propagated features: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}^{prop}_k = \|_{i=0}^h \tilde{\mathbf{A}}^i \mathbf{X}_k. \label{eq:client_feat_prop}\end{equation} Then we provide unbiased estimates of the sample mean and sample variance of features with labeled nodes for each class: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c} \mathbf{x}^{prop}_i, \boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c|-1} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c}(\mathbf{x}^{prop}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c)^2, \end{aligned} \label{eq:client_esimate_mean_var}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{V _{k,L ^c$ denotes the labeled nodes of class $c$ on client $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Note that we only estimate these statistics for categories with a number of nodes exceeding a certain threshold, which could be decided by each client locally.
elements.sent-2411-102: However, the labeled nodes would be a small part of the whole graph, and the estimation would not be accurate enough.
elements.sent-2411-103: We propose a Homophily-guided Reliable node Expansion strategy (HRE) as a plugin to further augment the estimation on homophily graphs.
elements.sent-2411-104: In HRE, we first conduct Label Propagation to obtain the soft labels $\tilde{\mathbf{y \in \mathbb{R ^C$ for each unlabeled node and then filter out unreliable soft labels.}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: We suppose a node's soft label is more reliable when 1) its prediction has higher confidence, 2) the category with its highest confidence has higher accumulated class homophily since it is more likely to share the same label with its neighbors, and 3) it has a larger node degree since more neighbors would reduce the variance.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus we select nodes $\mathcal{V _{k, r}$ with reliable soft labels which could be regarded as their true labels: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{V}_{k, r} = \{v_i | (v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k, U}) \wedge (\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_i(c') \geq f_{th}) \wedge (d(v_i) \geq d_{th}) \wedge (c' \in topK(H(c)))\}, c' = \arg\max_{0\leq i < C} \tilde{y}(i), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $topK(H)$ indicates the set of categories with top-K highest accumulated class homophily, $K, f_{th}$ and $d_{th}$ are hyper-parameters decided by each client locally.}

elements.figure-2411-005: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{figures/server_pseudo_graph.pdf}} \caption{The global pseudo-graph generation process on the server-side.} \label{fig:server}
elements.sent-2411-111: After clients obtain their estimated statistics of class-wise feature distribution $\{\boldsymbol{\mu _k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2 _k^c\}$, they upload them along with sample quantity of each class $\{N_k^c\}$ into the server.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: The server would aggregate them to recover the global statistics of class-wise feature distribution.
elements.sent-2411-113: Concretely, the global mean and global variance of class $c$ are reconstructed unbiasedly as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} N^c = \sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c, \boldsymbol{\mu}^c = \frac{1}{N^c}\sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2}^c = \frac{1}{N^c - m}(\sum_{k}^{m}(N_k^c - 1)\boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c + \sum_k^{m}N_k^c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c - \boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2). \end{aligned} \label{eq:server_mean_var}\end{equation} By aggregating uploaded statistics, the server could recover the global distribution of each class.
elements.sent-2411-114: Then these statistics would be used to instruct global pseudo-graph generation.
elements.sent-2411-115: Inspired by techniques from the graph condensation~\cite{jin2021graph, liu2022graph, yang2024does, xiao2024simple , we generate a small global pseudo-graph $G' = \{\mathbf{A ', \mathbf{X ', \mathbf{Y '\}$ by distribution alignment.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: By aligning the feature distribution of the pseudo-graph and aggregated global distribution class-wisely, the pseudo-graph can be optimized to capture the global information of graph data on each client.
elements.sent-2411-117: The generation process is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:server .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Specifically, we first pre-set the node labels $\mathbf{Y '$ in the global pseudo-graph where we set the number of class $c$'s nodes in the pseudo-graph as $pN^c$, where $p$ is a small fraction.}
elements.sent-2411-119: We initialize all node features by Gaussian noises and treat the node features $\mathbf{X '$ of the pseudo-graph as trainable parameters, along with a trainable link predictor $g_\theta$ to indicate the connectivity between two nodes in the pseudo-graph, represented as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{A}'_{i, j} = g_\theta(\mathbf{X}', \delta) , = \mathbb{I}(\delta \leq \sigma(\frac{g_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i \| \mathbf{x}_j)+ g_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j \| \mathbf{x}_i)}{2})), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A '$ is the adjacency matrix of the pseudo-graph, $\sigma$ denote the Sigmoid function, and $\delta$ is a hyper-parameter to keep sparsity.}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} A'_{i, j} = g_\theta(X', \delta) , = I(\delta \leq \sigma(\frac{g_\theta(x_i \| x_j)+ g_\theta(x_j \| x_i)}{2})), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-120: Following~\cite{xiao2024simple , to optimize the $\mathbf{X '$ and $g_\theta$, we first propagate the node feature with $\mathbf{A ' = g_\theta(\mathbf{X ', \delta)$ following the Eq.~\ref{eq:client_feat_prop , and obtain the class-wise sample mean and sample variance of the propagated node features $\mathbf{X '^{prop}$ in the pseudo-graph following Eq.~\ref{eq:client_esimate_mean_var as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\mu}'^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}'^c|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}'^c} \mathbf{x}'^{prop}_i, \boldsymbol{s^2}'^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}'^c|-1} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}'^c}(\mathbf{x}'^{prop}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}'^c)^2, \end{aligned} \label{eq:esimate_mean_var}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{V '^c$ denotes the nodes set of class $c$ in the pseudo-graph.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Then we calculate the alignment loss as: \begin{equation}L_{align} = \sum_{c=0}^{C-1} \lambda_c ((\boldsymbol{\mu}'^c - \boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2 + (\boldsymbol{s^2}'^c - \boldsymbol{s^2}^c)^2),\end{equation} where $\lambda_c$ represents the proportion of nodes belonging to class $c$ relative to the total number of nodes.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Personalized Training with Node Adaptive Distillation
elements.sent-2411-129: With the downloaded global pseudo-graph, each client trains its personalized model locally.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, existing personalized federated graph learning methods only focus on accuracy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-131: The imbalanced local data leads to a biased and unreliable model that only predicts well on major nodes which form a compact community but neglects the minority, reflected by deficient F1-macro value.
elements.sent-2411-132: To ensure better personalization (high overall accuracy) and generalization (high F1-macro), we propose a two-stage adaptive personalized model training.
elements.sent-2411-133: The intuition is to leverage both the global information in the pseudo-graph and personalization information in the local graph in a node-adaptive distillation manner.
elements.sent-2411-134: In the first stage, we train the local model only with the global pseudo-graph to obtain model $M_G$ by minimizing: \begin{equation}L_{1^{st}} = L_{ce}(M_G(\mathbf{A}', \mathbf{X}'), \mathbf{Y}'),\end{equation} where $M_G$ captures the global information and is not biased to local imbalanced data.
elements.sent-2411-135: Then we make a detached copy of $M_G$ as a fixed teacher model.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the second stage, client $k$ fine-tunes the $M_G$ with local graph data to obtain its personalized model $M_i$.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, since the local graph data are usually imbalanced, the fine-tuning (ft) would lead to a biased model that over-fits the major classes and forgets the knowledge of minor classes.
elements.sent-2411-138: To illustrate, we show that in Fig.~\ref{fig:citeseer_gap_H_major , the fine-tuned model shows performance gain in major classes (class 3 and class 5) but suffers performance drop in minor classes (class 4, class 2, and class 1).}
elements.sent-2411-139: Thus, the fine-tuned model suffers marginal overall accuracy improvement and low F1-macro value.
elements.sent-2411-140: Our solution is to take advantages of the global balanced knowledge of $M_G$ and local personalized knowledge in local graph data to train a better $M_i$ with the node adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-141: For nodes of major classes, we directly fine-tuned the model.
elements.sent-2411-142: For nodes of minor classes, we transfer the predictive ability of $M_G$ into $M_i$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Specifically, in the second stage, we train the model $M_i$ with $L_{2^{nd }$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} L_{2^{nd}} = L_{ft} + L_{dist}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $L_{ft}$ denotes the cross-entropy loss when fine-tuning on local graph data, and $L_{dist}$ is a weighted point-wise KL-divergence loss: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} L_{dist} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbf{X}_k} \gamma_i L_{kl}(M_i(\mathbf{x}_i), M_G(\mathbf{x}_i)), \label{eq:L_dist} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\gamma_i$ controls the source of the predictive ability in a node-wise manner.}
elements.sent-2411-144: A small $\gamma_i$ indicates the $M_i$ would mainly learn to predict the node $v_i$ with supervised fine-tuning, while a large $\gamma_i$ indicates the $M_i$ would imitate the prediction of copied teacher model $M_G$ on node $v_i$ additionally.
elements.sent-2411-145: To determine the value of $\gamma_i$ for each node, we utilize the soft labels obtained by Label Propagation in Sec.~\ref{sec:feat_dist_esti and $\mathbf{w_{dist }$ in Eq.~\ref{eq:w_dist : \begin{equation}\gamma_i = \beta \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_i \cdot \mathbf{w_{dist}}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Intuitively explained, 1) if a node is predicted to class $c$ where $w_{dist (c)$ is small (e.g. node $a$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:client_ada_per ), it is likely to belong to a major class with high homophily.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Thus $\gamma_i$ should be small and model $M_i$ learns to predict by supervised fine-tuning; 2) if a node is predicted to class $c$ where $w_{dist (c)$ is large or has a blended prediction (e.g. node $b$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:client_ada_per ), it is likely to belong to a minor class or with low homophily, which would be easy to neglect in supervised fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Thus $\gamma_i$ should be large to introduce corresponding global knowledge from $M_G$ additionally.

elements.sent-2411-152: After that, we aim to answer the following questions: Q1: Compared with other state-of-the-art methods, can our method achieve advantageous performance under different partition settings? elements.sent-2411-231: In this paper, we propose the first one-shot federated graph learning method which support model heterogeneity and Secure Aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-232: Specifically, we first estimate the statistics of class-wise feature distribution on each client.
elements.sent-2411-233: Then the server aggregate the class-wise statistics from clients and generate the global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-234: Moreover, we reveal the under-explored problems in personalized federated training and propose a two-stage personalized model training with node adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-235: Our method achieve SOTA performance under both accuracy and F1-macro metric on various datasets and setting.
elements.sent-2411-236: The future work is to extend our methods on more graphs with different properties (e.g. heterophilic and heterogeneous graphs) and more graph-centric tasks.. \bibliographystyle{./IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for breaking data silos in distributed private graphs data management.
elements.sent-2411-238: In practical scenarios involving complex and heterogeneous distributed graph data, personalized Federated Graph Learning (pFGL) aims to enhance model utility by training personalized models tailored to individual client needs, rather than relying on a universal global model.
elements.sent-2411-239: However, existing pFGL methods often require numerous communication rounds under heterogeneous client graphs, leading to significant security concerns and communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-240: While One-shot Federated Learning (OFL) addresses these issues by enabling collaboration in a single round, existing OFL methods are designed for image-based tasks and ineffective for graph data, leaving a critical gap in the field.
elements.sent-2411-241: Additionally, personalized models often suffer from bias, failing to generalize effectively to minority data.
elements.sent-2411-242: To address these challenges, we propose the first one-shot personalized federated graph learning method for node classification, compatible with the Secure Aggregation protocol for privacy preservation.
elements.sent-2411-243: Specifically, for effective graph learning in a single communication round, our method estimates and aggregates class-wise feature distribution statistics to construct a global pseudo-graph on the server, facilitating the training of a global graph model.
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, to mitigate bias, we introduce a two-stage personalized training approach that adaptively balances local personal information and global insights from the pseudo-graph, improving both personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-245: Extensive experiments conducted on 8 multi-scale graph datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across various settings.
elements.sent-2411-246: Graphs are widely employed to model complex relationships between entities across a variety of domains, such as recommendation systems~\cite{he2023simplifying, zhou2023contrastive, cai2023lightgcl , finance~\cite{yang2021financial, qiu2023default, hyun2023anti , and biomedicine~\cite{pfeifer2022gnn, qu2023graph, bang2023biomedical .}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: While various algorithms and models~\cite{gasteiger2018predict, iscen2019label, wu2020comprehensive, zhang2019heterogeneous have been proposed to analyze graph data effectively, most of them assume a centralized setting where graph data from different sources are aggregated together.}
elements.sent-2411-248: However, collecting and managing such graph data is often costly, impractical, and presents significant privacy risks in many cases.
elements.sent-2411-249: For example, in healthcare applications, sharing patient relation graphs across institutions can expose sensitive medical and personal identity information~\cite{voigt2017eu, ji2016graph, li2023private .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Similarly, in competing enterprises, graph data with potential commercial secrets are often strictly managed and stored in confidential databases~\cite{hubscher2022graph, fu2022federated , making centralized data collection and management infeasible.}
elements.sent-2411-251: To address the tension between the need for vast datasets and the growing demand for privacy protection, Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has emerged as a viable solution.
elements.sent-2411-252: FGL enables collaborative model training across distributed graph data without the need for data centralization, preserving privacy and improving security.
elements.sent-2411-253: While FGL has shown its potential in collaborative training, it faces two significant limitations: 1) Typical FGL struggles with personalizing models for joint improvement effectively due to heterogeneous graph data across clients.
elements.sent-2411-254: This heterogeneity arises from differences in both node and structural properties, with adjacent nodes influencing each other, further complicating personalization.
elements.sent-2411-255: These limitations can be mitigated by integrating personalized federated graph learning (pFGL) and one-shot federated learning (OFL), which offer novel solutions to improve communication efficiency and model personalization.
elements.figure-2411-020: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.98\columnwidth]{figures/posfgl.pdf}} \caption{The key limitations of existing methods. L1: Ineffectiveness for graph learning in one communication round. L2: Incompatibility with Secure Aggregation. L3: Imbalance between personalization and local generalization in personalization. The table details the limitations within L1 and L2, and our method stands out as the only method capable of supporting one-shot federated learning for graph data while accommodating model heterogeneity and Secure Aggregation.} \label{fig:limit_text}
elements.sent-2411-256: To address these challenges, we explore the integration of pFGL and OFL within the FGL paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-257: Methods of pFGL allow for personalized model training by focusing on individual client graph data.
elements.sent-2411-258: OFL reduces the communication overhead by enabling a single communication round, thereby circumventing security risks such as man-in-the-middle attacks and improve the communication efficiency~\cite{guha2019one, zhang2022dense .}
elements.sent-2411-259: However, both existing pFGL and OFL methods face challenges in FGL.
elements.sent-2411-260: Specifically, they exhibit the following limitations: L1: Ineffectiveness for graph learning in one communication round.
elements.sent-2411-261: Existing pFGL methods~\cite{baek2023personalized, li2024fedgta, zhu2024fedtad rely on iterative communication for model optimization, making them ineffective within one communication round.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Most methods also often fail to support heterogeneous models, as they primarily rely on aggregating model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-263: Meanwhile, existing OFL methods, which focus primarily on image-based tasks, are incompatible with graph data.
elements.sent-2411-264: The ensemble-based OFL methods~\cite{zhang2022dense, dai2024enhancing, zeng2024one can invert images from ensemble models, but struggle with graph data due to the inter-dependencies between nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-265: The distillation-based OFL also faces the problems of distilling inter-related graphs.
elements.sent-2411-267: Secure Aggregation is a widely used protocol~\cite{bonawitz2017practical, so2022lightsecagg designed to safeguard client privacy during the federated aggregation process.}
elements.sent-2411-268: However, most existing pFGL and OFL methods require the uploading of raw model parameters or side information (e.g. additional vectors in FedPUB and FedGTA) independently, which complicates the aggregation process (e.g. ensemble or cosine similarity calculation) and prevents the implementation of privacy-preserving weighted averages.
elements.sent-2411-269: L3: Imbalance between personalization and local generalization in personalization.
elements.sent-2411-270: Local graph data often exhibit a significant imbalance in both quantity and topology, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor_a .}
elements.sent-2411-271: Personalized models tend to be biased toward major classes (with larger quantities and high homophily that form compact communities) while neglecting minor classes with fewer samples and often surrounded by nodes from major classes.
elements.sent-2411-272: This imbalance leads to a degradation in overall model performance, particularly in underrepresented classes.
elements.sent-2411-273: A detailed empirical analysis is provided in Fig.\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor_b .}
elements.sent-2411-274: The left panel shows that existing personalized methods result in models heavily biased toward majority classes (class 3 and class 5, with a total of 180 samples) while failing to adequately represent minority classes (class 1 and class 2, with only 64 samples), highlighting the limitations of current approaches in handling imbalanced graph data.
elements.sent-2411-275: To overcome these limitations, we first propose a One-shot personalized Federated Graph Learning (O-pFGL) method.
elements.sent-2411-276: Our method supports model heterogeneity and integrates with the Secure Aggregation protocol to ensure privacy.
elements.sent-2411-277: Specifically, for L1 and L2, clients upload estimated feature distribution statistics instead of raw model parameters, and the server securely aggregates these statistics to generate a global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-278: This global pseudo-graph is subsequently utilized to train personalized models on clients, eliminating the need for direct model parameter exchanges and preventing model theft.
elements.sent-2411-279: This design streamlines the FGL process by reducing communication overhead and securely abstracting client-specific information into actionable insights in distributed and private graph data management.
elements.sent-2411-280: Furthermore, To address L3 and improve the personalization-generalization balance, we introduce a two-stage personalized training approach.
elements.sent-2411-281: In the first stage, clients train a global model based on the pseudo-graph, which captures global knowledge and serves as both a fixed teacher model and an initialization for subsequent fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-282: In the second stage, clients fine-tune the global model using their local graph data employing a node-adaptive distillation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-283: This mechanism leverages the global knowledge from the teacher model to counteract biases toward majority classes, ensuring effective learning for minority classes and achieving a robust balance between personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines through comprehensive experiments conducted on eight graph datasets across various settings, including different graph scales, partition strategies, learning paradigms, and evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-285: These experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method in achieving a one-shot personalized federated graph learning solution that balances privacy, efficiency, and personalization.
elements.sent-2411-286: To the best of our knowledge, we first investigate the one-shot personalized federated graph learning problem under both graph data heterogeneity and model heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-287: We propose a novel method that aggregates client statistics rather than model parameters to generate a global pseudo-graph, which is then used for two-stage personalized model training with node-adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-288: Our method is compatible with Secure Aggregation and protects the model's intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-289: We conduct comprehensive experiments on 8 multi-scale graph datasets.

elements.sent-2411-318: The corresponding labels are partitioned as $\mathbf{Y _{k, L}$ and $\mathbf{Y _{k, U}$, respectively.}}

elements.sent-2411-320: Nodes across all clients' local graphs are categorized into $C$ distinct classes.
elements.sent-2411-321: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) model~\cite{kipf2016semi $M$ takes the node feature matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and the normalized adjacency matrix $\hat{\mathbf{A }$ as input and outputs the logits $o \in \mathbb{R ^C$, which can be expressed as $o = M(\hat{\mathbf{A , \mathbf{X )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: For the prediction of a single node $v_i$, this is simplified to $o_i = M(\mathbf{x_i )$.}
elements.sent-2411-323: The cross-entropy loss is denoted by $L_{ce}$ and the KL divergence loss is denoted by $L_{kl}$.
elements.sent-2411-324: We formulate the problem of one-shot personalized federated graph learning for node classification as the following optimization objective: \begin{equation}\min \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} L(M_k, \mathbf{A}_k, \mathbf{X}_k, \mathbf{Y}_{k, U}), \label{eq:overall_obj_func}\end{equation} where $m$ represents the number of participating clients, each owning its local graph $G_k = (\mathcal{V _k, \mathcal{E _k)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-325: Each client trains its personalized model $M_k$.
elements.sent-2411-326: The objective in Eq.~\ref{eq:overall_obj_func aims to minimize the aggregated generalization loss $L$ on their unlabeled node set across all clients within a single upload-download communication round.}
elements.sent-2411-327: For a node $v_i \in \mathcal{V}$ in an undirected graph $G(\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$, the node degree $d(v_i)$ is the number of the edges connected to $v_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-329: For a labeled node $v_i \in \mathcal{V _L$ in an undirected graph $G(\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$, The node homophily $h_{node (v_i)$ quantifies the proportion of its labeled neighbors that share the same label $y_{v_i}$: \begin{equation}h_{node}(v_i) = \frac{\left|\{u | (u \in \mathcal{N}_{v_i} \cap \mathcal{V}_L) \text{and} (y_{v_i}=y_u) \}\right|}{|\mathcal{N}_{v_i} \cap \mathcal{V}_L|}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-330: In an undirected graph $G(V, E)$, The accumulated class homophily for class c is defined as: \begin{equation}H(c) = \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}_c^{G}} h_{node}(u), \label{eq:H(c)}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N _c^{G = \{v_i \in \mathcal{V _L | y_i = c\}$ represents the set of nodes in $G$ belonging to class $c$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-331: Note that Accumulated class homophily $H(c)$ considers both the number of nodes in class c and their topology.
elements.sent-2411-332: A higher $H(c)$ would indicate that nodes of class c are numerous and exhibit strong homophily, making them more predictable for a vanilla GNN model.
elements.sent-2411-333: Using Accumulated Class Homophily, we define the Class-aware Distillation Factor as: \begin{equation}w_{dist}(c) = \frac{1}{1+\log(H(c) + 1)}. \label{eq:w_dist}\end{equation} Classes with lower $H(c)$ require more distillation from a balanced global model.
elements.sent-2411-334: These factors are organized into a vector $\mathbf{w_{dist }\in \mathbb{R ^C$ where the $i$-th entry corresponds to $w_{dist (i)$ for class $i$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-339: Specifically, in the first step, we propose a homophily-guided reliable node expansion strategy as an augmentation to enhance the precision of the distribution statistics.

elements.sent-2411-343: The details are explained in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-344: To estimate the class-wise feature distribution locally, each client computes propagated node features using its local graph data.
elements.sent-2411-345: Specifically, on client $k$, the propagated features are calculated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}^{prop}_k = \|_{i=0}^h \tilde{\mathbf{A}}^i \mathbf{X}_k, \label{eq:client_feat_prop}\end{equation} where $h$ is the propagation depth and $\|$ represents the concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-346: Using the propagated features, we unbiasedly estimate the sample mean and sample variance of features for labeled nodes in each class: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c} \mathbf{x}^{prop}_i, \boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c|-1} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c}(\mathbf{x}^{prop}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c)^2, \end{aligned} \label{eq:client_esimate_mean_var}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{V _{k,L ^c$ denotes the labeled nodes of class $c$ on client $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-347: Note that statistics are computed only for classes with sufficient labeled nodes, determined by a client-specific threshold.
elements.sent-2411-348: However, due to the limited number of labeled nodes, the above estimation may lack accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-349: To address this, we propose a Homophily-guided Reliable node Expansion (HRE) strategy as a plugin to further augment the estimation process on homophilic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-350: In HRE, we first apply Label Propagation to obtain the soft labels $\tilde{\mathbf{y \in \mathbb{R ^C$ for unlabeled nodes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-351: We then identify reliable soft labels based on the following criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-353: The soft label has high confidence.
elements.sent-2411-355: The predicted class $c'$ has a high accumulated class homophily, making it likely that the node shares the same label with its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-357: Nodes with higher degrees are prioritized, as more neighbors reduce the variance in predictions.
elements.sent-2411-358: Thus, the set of reliable nodes $\mathcal{V _{k, r}$ with inferred true labels is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{V}_{k, r} = \{v_i | (v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k, U}) \wedge (\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_i(c') \geq f_{th}) \wedge (d(v_i) \geq d_{th}) \wedge (c' \in topK(H))\}, c' = \arg\max_{0\leq i < C} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}(i), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $topK(H)$ is the set of classes with the highest K-values of accumulated class homophily $H(c)$, $K, f_{th}$ and $d_{th}$ are client-defined hyper-parameters.}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} V_{k, r} = \{v_i | (v_i \in V_{k, U}) \wedge (\tilde{y}_i(c') \geq f_{th}) \wedge (d(v_i) \geq d_{th}) \wedge (c' \in topK(H))\}, c' = \arg\max_{0\leq i < C} \tilde{y}(i), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-359: The reliable nodes $\mathcal{V _{k, r}$ with their inferred labels expand the original labeled nodes set $\mathcal{V _{k, L}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Using the expanded labeled nodes set, the estimation of feature distribution could be augmented as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c \cup \mathcal{V}_{k, r}^c|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c \cup \mathcal{V}_{k, r}^c } \mathbf{x}^{prop}_i, \boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c \cup \mathcal{V}_{k, r}^c|-1} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{V}_{k,L}^c \cup \mathcal{V}_{k, r}^c }(\mathbf{x}^{prop}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c)^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{V _{k, r ^c$ represents the subset of reliable nodes in $\mathcal{V _{k, r}$ belonging to class $c$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-361: This augmented process ensures a more accurate and robust estimation of class-wise feature distributions by incorporating reliable nodes into the computation.
elements.figure-2411-023: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{figures/server_pseudo_graph.pdf}} \caption{The global pseudo-graph generation process on the server. $\boldsymbol{\mu}'$ and $\boldsymbol{s^2}'$ with different colors represent the class-wise sample mean and variance of the propagated node features in the global pseudo-graph, respectively.} \label{fig:server}
elements.sent-2411-362: After clients estimate the class-wise feature distribution statistics $\{\boldsymbol{\mu _k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2 _k^c\}$, they upload them along with sample quantity of each class $\{N_k^c\}$ into the server.}}
elements.sent-2411-363: The server aggregates them to recover the global class-wise feature distribution.
elements.sent-2411-364: Specifically, the global mean and global variance of class $c$ are computed unbiasedly as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} N^c = \sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c, \boldsymbol{\mu}^c = \frac{1}{N^c}\sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2}^c = \frac{1}{N^c - m}(\sum_{k}^{m}(N_k^c - 1)\boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c + \sum_k^{m}N_k^c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c - \boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2). \end{aligned} \label{eq:server_mean_var}\end{equation} Using the aggregated statistics, the server reconstructs the global distribution for each class, which is then used to guide the generation of a global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-365: Inspired by techniques from the graph condensation~\cite{jin2021graph, liu2022graph, yang2024does, xiao2024simple , we generate a small-size global pseudo-graph $G' = \{\mathbf{A ', \mathbf{X ', \mathbf{Y '\}$ by aligning its feature distribution with the aggregated global distribution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: This pseudo-graph serves as a representative of the overall graph data across all clients.
elements.sent-2411-367: To initialize $G'$, we first pre-set the node labels $\mathbf{Y '$ in the global pseudo-graph that the number of nodes for class $c$ in the pseudo-graph set to $\max\{1, pN^c\}$, where $p$ is a small fraction.}
elements.sent-2411-368: The node features $\mathbf{X '$ are initialized from Gaussian noise and treated as trainable parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-369: The adjacency matrix $\mathbf{A '$ is defined using a trainable link predictor $g_\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-370: The connectivity between node $i$ and $j$ is given by: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{A}'_{i, j} = g_\theta(\mathbf{X}', \delta) , = \mathbb{I}(\delta \leq \sigma(\frac{g_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i \| \mathbf{x}_j)+ g_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j \| \mathbf{x}_i)}{2})), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ denote the Sigmoid function, $\delta$ is a hyper-parameter to control sparsity, and $\|$ represents concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-371: Following~\cite{xiao2024simple , to optimize the $\mathbf{X '$ and $g_\theta$, we first propagate the node feature with $\mathbf{A '$ following the Eq.~\ref{eq:client_feat_prop .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-372: Then we calculate the class-wise sample mean $\boldsymbol{\mu '^c$ and sample variance $\boldsymbol{s^2 '^c$ of the propagated node features $\mathbf{X '^{prop}$ in the pseudo-graph following Eq.~\ref{eq:client_esimate_mean_var .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: Then we calculate the alignment loss: \begin{equation}L_{align} = \sum_{c=0}^{C-1} \lambda_c ((\boldsymbol{\mu}'^c - \boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2 + (\boldsymbol{s^2}'^c - \boldsymbol{s^2}^c)^2),\end{equation} where $\lambda_c$ represents the proportion of nodes belonging to class $c$ relative to the total number of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-374: To ensure the smoothness of the pseudo-graph, smoothness loss is applied: \begin{equation}L_{smooth} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i, j}\mathbf{A}'_{i,j}} \sum_{i, j}\mathbf{A}'_{i,j}\exp{(-\frac{\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2}{2})}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-380: Secure Aggregation enables the server to compute the sum of large, user-held data vectors securely, without accessing individual client contributions.
elements.sent-2411-381: This property aligns well with the aggregation process in Eq.~\ref{eq:server_mean_var , as it can be expressed as a series of weighted average operations.}
elements.sent-2411-382: The calculation of $N^c$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu ^c$ are inherently weighted averages.}
elements.sent-2411-383: Specifically: \begin{equation}N^c = \sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c, \ \boldsymbol{\mu}^c = \frac{1}{N^c}\sum_{k}^{m} N_k^c\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c, \\\end{equation} The calculation of $\boldsymbol{s^2 ^c$, while more complex, can also be decomposed into weighted average operations.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Decomposing the calculation of $\boldsymbol{s^2 ^c$ in Eq.~\ref{eq:server_mean_var , we have: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{s^2}^c = \frac{1}{N^c - m}(\sum_{k}^{m}(N_k^c - 1)\boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c + \sum_k^{m} N_k^c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c - \boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2), = \frac{\sum_{k}^{m}(N_k^c - 1)\boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c}{N^c - m} + \frac{\sum_k^{m}N_k^c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c)^2 - 2 \boldsymbol{\mu}^c \sum_k^{m}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c + N^c \sum_k^{m}(\boldsymbol{\mu}^c)^2}{N^c - m}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} Breaking this down further, the calculation involves the weighted averages of $(N_k^c -1)\boldsymbol{s^2 _k^c, N_k^c (\boldsymbol{\mu _k^c)^2$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu _k^c$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-385: Thus, the entire aggregation process for $\{\boldsymbol{\mu _k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2 _k^c, N_k^c\}$ can be conducted without revealing individual client data contributions, making it fully compatible with Secure Aggregation.}}
elements.sent-2411-386: Existing pFGL methods primarily focus on accuracy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-387: However, the imbalanced local graph data often results in biased models that perform well on major classes but neglect minority classes, as reflected by low F1-macro value.
elements.sent-2411-388: To achieve better personalization (high accuracy) and generalization (high F1-macro), we propose a two-stage adaptive personalized model training approach.
elements.sent-2411-389: The key idea is to leverage both the global information encapsulated in the global pseudo-graph and the local data, using a node-adaptive distillation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-390: In the first stage, each client trains a local model $M_G$ using only the global pseudo-graph, ensuring $M_G$ to capture global, balanced knowledge and avoid bias from the local data: \begin{equation}L_{1^{st}} = L_{ce}(M_G(\mathbf{A}', \mathbf{X}'), \mathbf{Y}'),\end{equation} A copy of the resulting $M_G$ is detached as a fixed teacher model for the next stage.
elements.sent-2411-391: In the second stage, client $k$ fine-tunes the $M_G$ with local graph data to derive a personalized model $M_i$.
elements.sent-2411-392: However, fine-tuning on imbalanced local graph data often leads to over-fitting on major classes while forgetting minor class knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-393: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:citeseer_gap_H_major , the fine-tuned model shows performance gain in major classes (class 3 and class 5) but suffers performance drop in minor classes (class 4, class 2, and class 1).}
elements.sent-2411-394: Thus, the fine-tuned model suffers limited overall accuracy improvement and low F1-macro value.
elements.sent-2411-395: To mitigate this, we propose to take advantage of the global knowledge of $M_G$ and local personalized knowledge in local graph data to train a better $M_i$ with the node adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-396: For nodes of major classes, the model directly learns through supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-397: For nodes of minor classes, $M_i$ additionally leverages the predictive ability of $M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-398: The training objective for the second stage is: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} L_{2^{nd}} = L_{ft} + L_{dist}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $L_{ft}$ is the cross-entropy loss for supervised fine-tuning on local data, and $L_{dist}$ is a weighted sum of point-wise KL-divergence loss that distills global knowledge from $M_G$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} L_{dist} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbf{X}_k} \gamma_i L_{kl}(M_i(\mathbf{x}_i), M_G(\mathbf{x}_i)), \label{eq:L_dist} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $\gamma_i$ is a node-specific weighting factor that controls the balance between local fine-tuning and global knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-399: To determine the value of $\gamma_i$ for each node, we utilize the soft labels $\tilde{\mathbf{y _i$ obtained by Label Propagation in Sec.~\ref{sec:feat_dist_esti and $\mathbf{w_{dist }$ in Eq.~\ref{eq:w_dist : \begin{equation}\gamma_i = \beta \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_i \cdot \mathbf{w_{dist}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-404: We first describe the experimental setup including graph datasets with various scales and training settings, baselines including pFGL and OFL methods, metrics for evaluation, and hyper-parameter configurations.
elements.sent-2411-405: Our experiments are designed to address the following key research questions: Q1: Compared with other state-of-the-art methods, can our method achieve advantageous performance under different partitions? Q2: On large-scale graphs and the increasing number of clients, can our method remain its performance advantageous? Q3: What are the primary contributors to the observed performance improvements? Q4: Can our method achieve superior performance under both data and model heterogeneity? Q5: Can our method sustain its advantages in inductive learning scenarios? Q6: How efficient is the communication of our method?.

elements.sent-2411-476: In this paper, we propose the first one-shot personalized federated graph learning method that supports model heterogeneity and Secure Aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-477: Specifically, our method first estimates class-wise feature distribution statistics on each client, augmented by our proposed HRE, and then aggregates these statistics on the server to generate the global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-478: To further enhance personalization and generalization, we propose a novel two-stage personalized model training with node-adaptive distillation, which balances local information and global insights from the global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-479: Extensive experiments demonstrate our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of both accuracy and F1-macro across various settings, significantly surpassing existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-480: Our method not only addresses critical limitations in integrating OFL and pFGL but also mitigates biases, improving the generalization, particularly for minorities.
elements.sent-2411-481: Future work will focus on extending our method to handle graphs with diverse properties (e.g., heterophilic and heterogeneous graphs) and more graph-centric tasks.. \bibliographystyle{./IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-482: However, existing pFGL methods often require numerous communication rounds under heterogeneous client graphs, leading to significant communication overhead and security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-483: While various algorithms and models~\cite{gasteiger2018predict, iscen2019label, wu2020comprehensive, zhang2019heterogeneous have been proposed to analyze graph data effectively, most of them assume a centralized setting where graph data from different sources are collected and managed in central databases~\cite{besta2022neural, tian2023world .}}
elements.sent-2411-484: However, collecting and managing such graph data is often costly, impractical, and poses significant privacy risks in many scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-485: To address the tension between the need for vast datasets and the growing demand for privacy protection, Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has emerged as a promising solution.
elements.sent-2411-486: FGL enables collaborative model training on distributed graph data without requiring centralized management~\cite{pan2023lumos, gu2023dynamic , thereby preserving privacy and enhancing security.}
elements.sent-2411-487: While FGL shows its potential in collaborative training, it faces two major limitations: (1) Typical FGL struggles to effectively personalize models for joint improvement due to the heterogeneity of graph data across clients.
elements.sent-2411-488: This heterogeneity arises from variations in both node and structural properties, with adjacent nodes influencing each other, further complicating personalization.
elements.sent-2411-489: The pFGL methods allow for personalized model training by focusing on individual client graph data.
elements.sent-2411-490: OFL reduces the communication overhead by enabling a single communication round, thereby improving communication efficiency~\cite{guha2019one, zhang2022dense and circumventing security risks such as man-in-the-middle attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-491: However, most pFGL and OFL methods require clients to upload raw model parameters or auxiliary information (e.g. additional vectors in FedPUB and FedGTA) independently, which complicates the aggregation process (e.g. additional ensemble or cosine similarity calculation) and hinders the implementation of privacy-preserving weighted averages.
elements.sent-2411-492: Specifically, for L1 and L2, clients upload estimated feature distribution statistics, augmented by our proposed homophily-guided reliable node expansion strategy, instead of raw model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-493: The server securely aggregates these statistics to generate a global pseudo-graph.
elements.sent-2411-494: These experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method in achieving a one-shot personalized federated graph learning solution that balances efficiency, privacy, and personalization.
elements.sent-2411-495: Our method is communication-efficient and significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines in all settings.
elements.sent-2411-496: To cope with heterogeneous graph data, GraphFL~\cite{wang2022graphfl adopts meta-learning~\cite{finn2017model for better generalization.}}
elements.sent-2411-497: FedPUB~\cite{baek2023personalized generates random graphs~\cite{holland1983stochastic to measure the similarity in model aggregation and conducts adaptive weight masks for better personalization.}}
elements.sent-2411-498: To better represent the minority in local graph data, FedSpray~\cite{fu2024federated learns local class-wise structure proxies to mitigate biased neighboring information.}
elements.sent-2411-499: Mainstream OFL methods can be classified into 3 categories.
elements.sent-2411-500: DENSE~\cite{zhang2022dense employs model inversion~\cite{yin2020dreaming from the ensemble model to generate images for distillation.}}
elements.sent-2411-501: FedOV~\cite{diao2023towards introduces placeholders in the model prediction layer.}
elements.sent-2411-502: IntactOFL~\cite{zeng2024one trains a MoE~\cite{jacobs1991adaptive network by generated images.}}
elements.sent-2411-503: FedDISC~\cite{yang2024exploring and FedDEO~\cite{yang2024feddeo leverage the pre-trained Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high to generate images and mitigate data heterogeneity.}}}

elements.sent-2411-534: Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for breaking data silos among distributed private graphs.
elements.sent-2411-535: In practical scenarios involving heterogeneous distributed graph data, personalized Federated Graph Learning (pFGL) aims to enhance model utility by training personalized models tailored to client needs.
elements.sent-2411-536: However, existing pFGL methods often require numerous communication rounds under heterogeneous graphs, leading to significant communication overhead and security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-537: While One-shot Federated Learning (OFL) enables collaboration in a single round, existing OFL methods are designed for image-centric tasks and ineffective for graph data, leaving a critical gap in the field.
elements.sent-2411-538: Additionally, personalized models derived from existing methods suffer from bias, failing to effectively generalize to the minority.
elements.sent-2411-539: To address these challenges, we propose the first One-shot personalized Federated Graph Learning method (O-pFGL) for node classification, compatible with Secure Aggregation protocols for privacy preservation.
elements.sent-2411-540: Specifically, for effective graph learning in one communication round, our method estimates and aggregates class-wise feature distribution statistics to construct a global pseudo-graph on the server, facilitating the training of a global graph model.
elements.sent-2411-541: To mitigate bias, we introduce a two-stage personalized training approach that adaptively balances local personal information and global insights from the pseudo-graph, improving both personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-542: Extensive experiments on 12 multi-scale graph datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across various settings.
elements.sent-2411-543: Graphs are widely employed to model complex relationships between entities across a variety of domains~\cite{he2023simplifying, hyun2023anti, bang2023biomedical .}
elements.sent-2411-544: While various algorithms and models~\cite{gasteiger2018predict, iscen2019label, wu2020comprehensive have been proposed, most of them assume that graph data from different sources are managed centrally.}
elements.sent-2411-545: However, collecting and managing such graph data is often costly, impractical, and poses significant privacy risks in many sensitive scenarios~\cite{fu2022federated .}

elements.sent-2411-546: To address the tension between the need for vast datasets and the growing demand for privacy protection, Federated Graph Learning (FGL) has emerged as a promising solution, which enables collaborative training on distributed graph data without requiring centralized management~\cite{pan2023lumos, gu2023dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-547: While FGL shows its potential, it faces two major limitations: (1) \uline{Typical FGL struggles to effectively personalize models for joint improvement across heterogeneous clients' graph data.}
elements.sent-2411-548: This heterogeneity arises from variations of both node and structural properties, with adjacent nodes influencing each other, further complicating personalization.
elements.sent-2411-549: To address these challenges, we explore the integration of pFGL and OFL.
elements.sent-2411-550: OFL methods limit communication to a single round, thereby reducing the communication overhead and circumventing security risks such as man-in-the-middle attacks~\cite{guha2019one, zhang2022dense .}
elements.sent-2411-551: Existing pFGL methods~\cite{baek2023personalized, li2024fedgta, zhu2024fedtad rely on iterative communication for optimization, making them ineffective within one communication round.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Most methods also fail to support heterogeneous models, as they primarily rely on aggregating model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-553: Meanwhile, existing OFL methods, which focus primarily on image-centric tasks, are incompatible with graph data.
elements.sent-2411-554: The ensemble-based OFL methods~\cite{zhang2022dense, dai2024enhancing can invert images from ensemble models, but struggle with graph data due to the inter-dependencies between nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-555: The distillation-based OFL faces the problems of distilling inter-related graphs.
elements.sent-2411-556: However, most pFGL and OFL methods require clients to upload raw model parameters or auxiliary information independently, which complicates the aggregation process (e.g. ensemble or similarity calculation) and hinders the implementation of privacy-preserving weighted averages.
elements.sent-2411-557: Local graph data often exhibit a significant imbalance in both quantity and topology, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor_a .}
elements.sent-2411-558: Personalized models tend to be biased toward major classes (with larger quantities and high homophily to form compact communities), while neglecting minor classes with fewer samples and often surrounded by nodes from major classes.
elements.sent-2411-559: A detailed empirical analysis is provided in Figure\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor_b , which shows that existing personalized methods result in heavy bias toward majority classes (class 3 and class 5, with a total of 180 samples) while failing to adequately represent minority classes (class 1 and class 2, with only 64 samples), highlighting the limitations of current methods in handling imbalanced graph data.}
elements.sent-2411-560: To overcome these limitations, we propose a One-shot personalized Federated Graph Learning method (O-pFGL).
elements.sent-2411-561: Our method supports model heterogeneity and Secure Aggregation protocols for privacy preservation.
elements.sent-2411-562: The server securely aggregates these statistics to generate a global pseudo-graph, which is subsequently utilized to train personalized models on clients, eliminating the need for direct model parameter exchanges and preventing model theft.
elements.sent-2411-563: To address L3, we propose a two-stage personalized training approach.
elements.sent-2411-564: In the first stage, clients train a global model based on the pseudo-graph, which serves as both a fixed teacher capturing global knowledge and a start-point for subsequent fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-565: In the second stage, clients fine-tune the global model on local graph data with the node-adaptive distillation, which leverages the global knowledge from the teacher model to counteract biases toward majority, ensuring effective learning for minority and achieving a robust balance between personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-566: Our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines through comprehensive experiments across various settings, including different graph scales, partition strategies, learning paradigms, and evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-567: Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method in achieving a one-shot personalized federated graph learning solution that balances efficiency, privacy, and personalization.
elements.sent-2411-569: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the one-shot personalized federated graph learning problem under both graph data heterogeneity and model heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-570: We propose a novel method that aggregates client statistics rather than model parameters to generate a global pseudo-graph, which is used for two-stage personalized training with node-adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-571: Our method is compatible with Secure Aggregation and protects models' intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-572: We conduct extensive experiments on 12 multi-scale graph datasets across various settings and demonstrate the superiority of our method.

elements.sent-2411-736: The communication costs of our method are independent of the model size.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Experimental Results on Link Prediction Task
elements.sent-2411-757: To evaluate the effectiveness of the methods of node representation learning, we perform the link prediction task.
elements.sent-2411-758: We first perform the node classification task to pre-train a GCN as the feature encoder with a classification head to generate distinguishable node representation and then fine-tune the feature encoder for link prediction.
elements.sent-2411-759: The experimental results (take AUC as our metrics) are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:link_pred , which shows that our method learns better node representation and achieves the best on the subsequent link prediction task while having less communication cost.}
elements.sent-2411-760: A detailed empirical analysis is provided in Figure~\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor_b , which shows that existing personalized methods result in heavy bias toward majority classes (class 3 and class 5, with a total of 180 samples) while failing to adequately represent minority classes (class 1 and class 2, with only 64 samples), highlighting the limitations of current methods in handling imbalanced graph data.}
elements.sent-2411-761: Node features matrix is $\mathbf{X _k = \{\mathbf{x _1, ..., \mathbf{x _{n_k}\} \in \mathbb{R ^{|\mathcal{V _k| \times f}$, where $f$ denotes the feature dimension and $\mathbf{x _i$ is the feature of node $v_i$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-762: The adjacency matrix is $\mathbf{A _k$ and augmented normailzied adjacency matrix is $\tilde{\mathbf{A _k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-763: We formulate the one-shot personalized federated graph learning for node classification problem as: \begin{equation}\min \sum_{k=1}^{m} L(M_k(\mathbf{A}_k, \mathbf{X}_k), \mathbf{Y}_{k, U}), \label{eq:overall_obj_func}\end{equation} This objective aims to minimize the aggregated generalization loss $L$ on their unlabeled node set with personalized models $M_k$ across all clients within a single upload-download communication round.
elements.sent-2411-764: Specifically, on client $k$, the propagated features are: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}^{prop}_k = \|_{i=0}^h \tilde{\mathbf{A}}_k^i \mathbf{X}_k, \label{eq:client_feat_prop}\end{equation} where $h$ is the propagation depth and $\|$ represents the concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-765: Client $k$ fine-tunes $M_G$ with local graph data to derive a personalized model $M_k$.
elements.sent-2411-766: However, fine-tuning on imbalanced local graph data often leads to over-fitting on major classes while forgetting minor classes.
elements.sent-2411-767: To mitigate this, we propose to utilize the global knowledge of $M_G$ and personalized knowledge in local graph data to train a better $M_k$ with the node adaptive distillation.
elements.sent-2411-768: For nodes of minor classes, $M_k$ additionally distills the predictive ability from $M_G$.
elements.sent-2411-769: The training objective for the second stage is to minimize: \begin{equation}L_{2^{nd}} = L_{ft} + L_{dist},\end{equation} where $L_{ft}$ is the cross-entropy loss for supervised fine-tuning on local training data, and $L_{dist}$ is the weighted sum of point-wise KL divergence loss to distill global knowledge from $M_G$ into $M_k$: \begin{equation}L_{dist} = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbf{X}_k} \gamma_i L_{kl}(M_k(\mathbf{A}_k, \mathbf{x}_i), M_G(\mathbf{A}_k, \mathbf{x}_i)), \label{eq:L_dist}\end{equation} $\gamma_i$ is a node-specific weighting factor that controls the balance between local fine-tuning and global knowledge distillation.

elements.sent-2411-786: While One-shot Federated Learning (OFL) enables collaboration in a single round, existing OFL methods are designed for image-centric tasks and are ineffective for graph data, leaving a critical gap in the field.

Our method effectively mitigates the performance gap.} \label{fig:citeseer_major_minor}
elements.sent-2411-787: While FGL shows its potential, it faces two major limitations: (1) Typical FGL struggles to effectively personalize models for joint improvement across heterogeneous clients' graph data~\cite{baek2023personalized .}
elements.sent-2411-788: Meanwhile, existing OFL methods are incompatible with graph data since they focus primarily on image-centric tasks.
elements.sent-2411-789: The ensemble-based OFL methods~\cite{zhang2022dense, dai2024enhancing can generate images from ensemble models, but struggle with graph data due to the inter-dependencies between nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-790: The generative-based OFL methods~\cite{heinbaugh2023data, yang2024feddeo, yang2024exploring rely on pre-trained generative models (e.g., Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high ), which are impractical for graph generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-791: Secure Aggregation is a widely used technique~\cite{bonawitz2017practical, so2022lightsecagg designed to safeguard client privacy during the federated aggregation process.}
elements.sent-2411-792: However, most pFGL and OFL methods require clients to upload raw model parameters or auxiliary information independently, which complicates the aggregation process (e.g., ensemble or similarity calculation) and hinders the implementation of privacy-preserving weighted averages.
elements.sent-2411-793: L3: Imbalance between personalization and local generalization.
elements.sent-2411-794: This imbalance leads to a degradation in model performance, particularly in underrepresented classes.
elements.sent-2411-795: In the first stage, clients train a global generalized model based on the pseudo-graph, which serves as both a fixed teacher capturing global knowledge and a start-point for subsequent fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-796: In the second stage, clients fine-tune the global model on local graph data with the node-adaptive distillation, which leverages the global knowledge from the teacher model to counteract biases toward the majority, ensuring effective learning for the minority and achieving a robust balance between personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-797: Our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines through comprehensive experiments across various settings, including different graph scales, partition strategies, learning paradigms, and model architectures.
elements.sent-2411-798: To sum up, our contributions are listed as follows: Bridge the Gap.
elements.sent-2411-799: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the one-shot personalized federated graph learning problem under both graph data heterogeneity and model heterogeneity.. Novel Solution.
elements.sent-2411-800: Our method is compatible with Secure Aggregation and protects models' intellectual property.. SOTA Performance.
elements.sent-2411-801: From the graph level~\cite{xie2021federated, tan2023federated , each client possesses multiple independent graphs (e.g., molecular graphs) and targets at graph-level tasks.}
