elements.subsubsection-2411-017: Critical Analysis of Current Related Work
elements.paragraph-2411-033: Existing FGL methods require numerous communication rounds.
elements.sent-2411-983: Existing FGL methods typically follow the conventional paradigm in which the server adaptively aggregates client models.
elements.sent-2411-984: Under practical non-IID scenarios, numerous studies have shown that this paradigm can lead to issues such as weight divergence~\cite{zhao2018federated , client drift~\cite{karimireddy2020scaffold , and biases and conflicts among client models~\cite{zhu2021data .}}}
elements.sent-2411-985: These challenges necessitate extensive communication rounds to achieve acceptable performance.
elements.sent-2411-986: Specialized FGL methods (e.g., FedPUB, FedTAD, FedGTA) necessitate unbiased client models with strong predictive abilities for various tasks, including proxy embedding computation, knowledge distillation, and label propagation.
elements.sent-2411-987: These methods require significant communication rounds to resolve these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-988: FedSpray additionally optimizes the structure proxy, which demands increased communication rounds for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-989: FedLoG needs to optimize the global synthetic data, which also needs iterative communications.
elements.paragraph-2411-034: Existing OFL methods are not designed for graph data.
elements.sent-2411-990: Existing OFL methods are mostly designed for image data.
elements.sent-2411-991: When dealing with graph data, these methods are incompatible or impractical.
elements.sent-2411-992: To be specific, ensemble-based methods such as DENSE train a generator to generate pseudo images via model inversion.
elements.sent-2411-993: However, it's not perfectly compatible with graph data generation since it can hardly capture the fine-grained structural information of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-994: Distillation-based methods generate distilled data on clients, and the server assembles the distilled data.
elements.sent-2411-995: However, the nodes in a graph are not independent.
elements.sent-2411-996: It's tricky to assemble generated graphs from clients on the server side.
elements.sent-2411-997: Generative-based methods use VAE or pretrained Stable Diffusion, which cannot generate graph data.
elements.sent-2411-998: Thus, there are not OFL methods that fit perfectly for graph data.
elements.paragraph-2411-035: Most FGL and OFL methods are not compatible with Secure Aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-999: Secure Aggregation protocols are widely used in federated learning, which enable the server to compute the sum of large, user-held data vectors securely, without accessing the individual client contributions.
elements.sent-2411-1000: Thus, the client's uploaded models or other information could be protected from third-party and the curious server.
elements.sent-2411-1001: The application of Secure Aggregation is based on the fact that only weighted average operations are conducted in vanilla federated learning methods like FedAvg.
elements.sent-2411-1002: However, the recent federated graph learning methods have become complicated.
elements.sent-2411-1003: The server needs to conduct other operations, including ensemble (e.g., FedTAD, DENSE, and Co-Boost), data-free distillation (e.g., FedTAD, DENSE, and Co-Boost), functional embedding calculation (e.g., FedPUB), complex similarity calculation (e.g., FedGTA), and others (e.g., FedLoG).
elements.sent-2411-1004: The server must access every client uploading to conduct these operations.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Thus, they are not compatible with Secure Aggregation and third-party, and the curious server could easily access each client's uploaded information.
elements.sent-2411-1006: We provide a detailed description of the baseline methods in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-1007: Standalone is a non-federated learning method where each client trains its model only use its local graph data, without communication.
elements.sent-2411-1008: FedAvg is the fundamental federated learning method where each client trains its local model and upload to the server.
elements.sent-2411-1009: The server weighted average the client models' parameters to obtain the global model and then distributes the global model to clients.
elements.sent-2411-1010: In our experiments, we limit the upload-download communication round to one round.
elements.sent-2411-1011: For better performance, we perform additional finetuning on local graph data after downloading the global model.
elements.sent-2411-1012: FedPUB is a personalized federated graph learning method.
elements.sent-2411-1013: FedPUB computes similarities between local models via functional embeddings derived from random graphs generated in the server.
elements.sent-2411-1014: The similarities are used for weighted average in personalized aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Additionally, FedPUB employs a personalized mask for each client model to selectively upload parameters.
elements.sent-2411-1016: FedGTA is a personalized federated graph learning method.
elements.sent-2411-1017: FedGTA compute and upload the local smoothing confidence and mixed moments of neighbor features to the server to facilitate the weighted average in personalized aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-1018: FedTAD computes the class-wise knowledge reliability scores on each client.
elements.sent-2411-1019: The scores, along with local models, are uploaded to the server for data-free knowledge distillation from the ensemble of local models to the global model or personalized model.
elements.sent-2411-1020: For better performance, we perform additional finetuning on local graph data after downloading the model.
elements.sent-2411-1021: FedSpray focuses on the minor classes.
elements.sent-2411-1022: It optimizes the structure proxy along with the local model on each client.
elements.sent-2411-1023: FedSpray uploads and aggregate the structure proxy, which is used in local model training to prevent overfitting and ensure the generalization to the minor classes.
elements.sent-2411-1024: FedLoG focuses on the local generalization of model training.
elements.sent-2411-1025: Each client pretrains a prompt generator and synthesizes nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1026: The server aggregates the model and synthesized nodes and generates global synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-1027: Then, each client trains its model on local graph data and global synthetic data to derive a generalized model.
elements.sent-2411-1028: DENSE is a one-shot federated learning method.
elements.sent-2411-1029: Each client uploads its trained local model to the server.
elements.sent-2411-1030: The server ensembles the client models to generate pseudo data via model inversion, which are used to distill knowledge from client models to the global model or personalized models.
elements.sent-2411-1031: For better performance, we perform additional finetuning on local graph data after downloading the data.
elements.sent-2411-1032: Note that it's designed for the image classification task.
elements.sent-2411-1033: In our node classification task on graphs, the generators in model inversion on the server-side are trained to generate node features, and the topology structure is constructed using the $K$-Nearest Neighbors strategy as outlined in~\cite{zhu2024fedtad .}
elements.sent-2411-1034: Co-Boost is a one-shot federated learning method.
elements.sent-2411-1036: The server ensembles the client models with learnable weights.
elements.sent-2411-1037: The ensemble weights and generated pseudo data are optimized alternately.
elements.subsection-2411-024: Our method's Advantages in One Communication Round
elements.sent-2411-1038: Most existing methods meet issues like weight divergence, client drift, and discrepancies and conflicts between client models raised from non-IID scenarios and fewer communication rounds.
elements.sent-2411-1039: However, our method is robust to non-IID and can even achieve optimal performance in one round on average compared with multi-round federated learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-1040: In our method, we do not aggregate the model parameters (which is a major source of information loss in other methods).
elements.sent-2411-1041: Rather, the aggregation process in our method is lossless and unbiased regardless of the data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-1042: Thus, we do not need multi-round communications to tackle the issues met by the existing methods above.
elements.sent-2411-1043: Also, our method enhances the local generalization of the client models by considering both local and global information, which further boosts our method's performance.

elements.sent-2411-1053: However, both existing pFGL and OFL methods face the following challenges and limitations: L1: Ineffectiveness for graph learning in one communication round under heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-1054: Existing pFGL methods~\cite{baek2023personalized, li2024fedgta rely on iterative communication, making them ineffective within one communication round, especially under heterogeneous data.}
elements.sent-2411-1055: A recent work, GHOST~\cite{qianghost , explores training a generalized model in one-shot communication.}
elements.sent-2411-1056: But it's not robust under severe non-IIDness and does not address personalization issues, as confirmed in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-1057: Furthermore, such parameter-aggregation-based methods inherently fail to support heterogeneous client models.
elements.sent-2411-1058: Meanwhile, existing OFL methods are incompatible with graph data, as they primarily focus on image-centric tasks.
elements.sent-2411-1059: Ensemble-based OFL methods~\cite{zhang2022dense, dai2024enhancing can generate images from ensemble models but struggle with graph data due to the inter-dependency between nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-1060: Distillation-based OFL faces problems distilling interrelated graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1061: Generative-based OFL methods~\cite{heinbaugh2023data, yang2024feddeo, yang2024exploring rely on pre-trained generative models (e.g., Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high ), which are impractical for graph generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-1062: Secure Aggregation is a widely used technique~\cite{bonawitz2017practical designed to safeguard client privacy during uploading and aggregation in federated learning.}
elements.sent-2411-1063: However, most existing methods require clients to upload raw model parameters or auxiliary information independently, which complicates the process (e.g., similarity calculation or model ensemble) and hinders the implementation of privacy-preserving uploading and aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-1064: The individual information could be revealed in the communication~\cite{zhu2024evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-1065: Local graph data often exhibits a significant imbalance in both quantity and topology.
elements.sent-2411-1066: Personalized models tend to be biased toward major classes (e.g., with larger quantities and high homophily to form compact communities), while neglecting minor classes with fewer samples and often surrounded by nodes from major classes.
elements.sent-2411-1067: A detailed empirical analysis is provided in Figure~\ref{fig:citeseer_major_minor , which shows that existing methods result in heavy bias toward majority classes (class 3 and class 5, with a total of 180 samples) while failing to adequately represent minority classes (class 1 and class 2, with only 64 samples), highlighting the limitations of current methods in handling imbalanced graph data.}
elements.sent-2411-1068: The server securely aggregates these statistics to generate a global surrogate graph, which is subsequently utilized to train personalized models on clients, eliminating the need for direct model parameter exchanges and further protecting the client's model intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-1069: In the first stage, clients train a global generalized model based on the surrogate graph, which serves as both a fixed teacher capturing global knowledge and a starting point for subsequent fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-1070: Our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art through comprehensive experiments across various settings, including different graph scales, homophilic and heterophilic, learning paradigms, and model architectures.
elements.sent-2411-1071: Experimental results demonstrate our method's superiority in achieving a personalization-generalization balanced model within one-shot communication, considering both data and model privacy.
elements.sent-2411-1072: To sum up, our contributions are: New Problem: One-shot Personalized FGL.
elements.sent-2411-1073: We are the first to tackle the problem of personalization in one-shot federated graph learning under dual challenges of data and model heterogeneity, and ensuring both privacy.
elements.sent-2411-1074: Novel Solution: Our solution firstly aggregates client statistics rather than model parameters to generate a global surrogate graph that captures global distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1075: Leveraging this, we then introduce two-stage personalized training with node-adaptive distillation to strike an effective balance between personalization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-1076: SOTA Performance: We conduct extensive experiments on 14 diverse real-world graph datasets and demonstrate that our method consistently and significantly outperforms existing baselines across various settings.

elements.section-2411-011: Compatibility with Classical Secure Aggregation Implementation
elements.sent-2411-1179: The uploading and aggregation process of our method is compatible with the classical Secure Aggregation implementation since it can be decomposed into a series of weighted average operations.
elements.sent-2411-1180: We show the pseudo-code of a classical implementation in Algorithm~\ref{alg:secure_agg_simple .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Implementation of Secure Aggregation for Our Federated Aggregation} \label{alg:secure_agg_simple} Input: A set of $m$ clients $\{Client_1, \dots, Client_m\}$. Each client $k$ has local data to compute $N_k^c, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c, \boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c$. Output: $N^c, \boldsymbol{\mu}^c, \boldsymbol{s^2}^c$. \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE \textit{// Assume a key exchange protocol (e.g., Diffie-Hellman~\cite{li2010research}) has been run. Each pair of clients $(k, j)$ now shares a common secret key $s_{k,j}$.} \STATE // --- Masking on each Client $k$ (in parallel) --- \STATE \textit{// Construct the private data vector $\boldsymbol{U}_k$} \STATE $A_k^c \leftarrow N_k^c$; $\boldsymbol{B}_k^c \leftarrow N_k^c \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c$; $\boldsymbol{C}_k^c \leftarrow (N_k^c - 1)\boldsymbol{s^2}_k^c$; $\boldsymbol{D}_k^c \leftarrow N_k^c(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^c)^2$ \STATE $\boldsymbol{U}_k \leftarrow \text{concatenate}(A_k^c, \boldsymbol{B}_k^c, \boldsymbol{C}_k^c, \boldsymbol{D}_k^c)$ \STATE // Generate masks \STATE $\boldsymbol{b}_k \leftarrow \text{PRNG}(\text{random\_seed()})$ \COMMENT{Store personal mask $\boldsymbol{b}_k$} \STATE $\boldsymbol{p}_k \leftarrow \boldsymbol{0}$ \FOR{$j=1$ to $m$} \IF{$j \neq k$} \STATE $\boldsymbol{r}_{k,j} \leftarrow \text{PRNG}(s_{k,j})$ \COMMENT{$s_{k,j}$ is the pre-shared secret with client $j$} \IF{$k < j$} \STATE $\boldsymbol{p}_k \leftarrow \boldsymbol{p}_k + \boldsymbol{r}_{k,j}$ \ELSE \STATE $\boldsymbol{p}_k \leftarrow \boldsymbol{p}_k - \boldsymbol{r}_{k,j}$ \ENDIF \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE // Create and send the final masked vector \STATE $\boldsymbol{y}_k \leftarrow \boldsymbol{U}_k + \boldsymbol{b}_k + \boldsymbol{p}_k$ \STATE Each client $k$ sends its masked vector $\boldsymbol{y}_k$ to the Server. \STATE // --- Aggregation on the Server --- \STATE The Server collects all masked vectors $\{\boldsymbol{y}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{y}_m\}$ and sums them: $\boldsymbol{Y} \leftarrow \sum_{k=1}^{m} \boldsymbol{y}_k$. \STATE The Server collects all personal masks $\{\boldsymbol{b}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{b}_m\}$ from the clients. \STATE The Server sums the personal masks: $\boldsymbol{B}_{sum} \leftarrow \sum_{k=1}^{m} \boldsymbol{b}_k$. \STATE The Server computes the true sum: $\boldsymbol{U}_{sum} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{Y} - \boldsymbol{B}_{sum}$. \STATE // Final Statistics Computation by Server \STATE $(A_{sum}^c, \boldsymbol{B}_{sum}^c, \boldsymbol{C}_{sum}^c, \boldsymbol{D}_{sum}^c) \leftarrow \text{parse}(\boldsymbol{U}_{sum})$ \STATE $N^c \leftarrow A_{sum}^c$ \STATE $\boldsymbol{\mu}^c \leftarrow \boldsymbol{B}_{sum}^c / N^c$ \STATE $\Sigma_{within} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{C}_{sum}^c$ \STATE $\Sigma_{between} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{D}_{sum}^c - (\boldsymbol{B}_{sum}^c)^2 / N^c$ \STATE $\boldsymbol{s^2}^c \leftarrow \frac{1}{N^c - m} (\Sigma_{within} + \Sigma_{between})$ \STATE return $N^c, \boldsymbol{\mu}^c, \boldsymbol{s^2}^c$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-1219: For FedGM, each client needs to conduct gradient matching to synthesize a graph.
elements.sent-2411-1220: The server needs to aggregate and infer the synthesized graphs with uploaded $\text{PGE}$ from $N$ clients.
elements.sent-2411-1221: For GHOST, the computational complexity is very high.
elements.sent-2411-1222: Each client needs to construct a pseudo graph with the same size as the local graph by feature matrix multiply.
elements.sent-2411-1223: This operation is $O(n^2d)$, which would be impractical on large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-1224: On the server side, to integrate the knowledge and avoid catastrophic forgetting, the server needs to traverse every edge in the pseudo graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1225: The number of edges is approximately the same order as the number of edges in the latent global graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1226: Thus, this process is with $O(m(fd+d^2))$ complexity, also impractical under large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-1227: The computational complexity on clients in our method is kept as least as possible, the same as the vanilla FedAvg and Standalone.

elements.sent-2411-1236: Recent works~\cite{xie2021federated, tan2023federated, tan2024fedssp, fu2025virtual mainly focus on the intrinsic heterogeneity among graphs from different clients.}

elements.sent-2411-1238: GHOST~\cite{qianghost trains a proxy model to align with local graph data, and the server integrates the knowledge from the uploaded proxy model.}
elements.subsection-2411-038: Critical Analysis of Current Related Work
elements.paragraph-2411-038: Most FGL methods are ineffective within one-shot communication under data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-1239: FedGM and GHOST offer methods for training a generalized model in one-shot communication.
elements.sent-2411-1240: However, they are not robust to non-IID scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-1241: FedGM adopts gradient matching on each client, which is unstable under biased and heterogeneous local graph data.
elements.sent-2411-1242: GHOST constructs the pseudo graph with the aligned proxy model, but the proxy model is still biased and heterogeneous under non-IID client graph data.
elements.sent-2411-1243: And the performance degrades easily in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-1244: Moreover, neither of them is scalable with respect to the time complexity and memory consumption.
elements.sent-2411-1245: The high complexity hinders them to adapt to larger graphs effectively.
elements.paragraph-2411-039: Most OFL methods are not designed for graph data.
elements.sent-2411-1246: Thus, there are no OFL methods that fit perfectly for graph data.

elements.sent-2411-1247: Standalone is a non-federated learning method where each client trains its model only using its local graph data, without communication.
elements.sent-2411-1248: FedAvg is the fundamental federated learning method where each client trains its local model and uploads it to the server.
elements.sent-2411-1249: The server weights averages the client models' parameters to obtain the global model and then distributes the global model to clients.
elements.sent-2411-1250: For better performance, we perform additional fine-tuning on local graph data after downloading the global model.
elements.sent-2411-1251: FedPUB computes similarities between local models via functional embeddings derived from random graphs generated on the server.
elements.sent-2411-1252: FedGTA computes and uploads the local smoothing confidence and mixed moments of neighbor features to the server to facilitate the weighted average in personalized aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-1253: For better performance, we perform additional fine-tuning on local graph data after downloading the model.
elements.sent-2411-1254: FedSpray uploads and aggregates the structure proxy, which is used in local model training to prevent overfitting and ensure the generalization to the minor classes.
elements.sent-2411-1255: FedGM adopts gradient matching to condense the synthesized graph on each client.
elements.sent-2411-1256: Each client uploads the synthesized graphs or gradients to the server, and the server aggregates them.
elements.sent-2411-1257: In our experiment, we follow the setting from the original paper to conduct one-round communication and use aggregated synthesized graphs to aid the model training.
elements.sent-2411-1258: GHOST aims to train a generalized model in one-shot communication.
elements.sent-2411-1259: Each client constructs a pseudo graph and trains a proxy model by aligning the pseudo graph and the real local graph.
elements.sent-2411-1260: Each client then uploads the proxy model.
elements.sent-2411-1261: The server generates the pseudo-graph with each uploaded proxy model and trains a global model with crucial parameters stabilized to alleviate catastrophic forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-1262: The server ensembles the client models to generate pseudo data via model inversion, which is used to distill knowledge from client models to the global model or personalized models.
elements.sent-2411-1263: For better performance, we perform additional fine-tuning on local graph data after downloading the data.
